react-stable-timeline.title: @mogiyoon/react-stable-timeline
react-stable-timeline.subtitle: A React Timeline Library Where Rows Stay Stable When You Pan or Zoom
react-stable-timeline.description:
Viewport-based stacking algorithms recompute rows from only the currently visible items, so the same event lands on row 3 at one pan position and row 1 at another. This library packs rows from the full dataset, so panning never reshuffles rows.

Distributed as an npm package built with React 18+, TypeScript, and tsup. Label widths are measured with Canvas2D measureText so Hangul, CJK, and mixed-script text are sized correctly, eliminating the label collisions that character-count estimates produce.

overview.introduction: A React timeline component library where rows stay stable through pan and zoom
overview.features: First-fit row packing, Canvas2D label measurement, Photoshop-style zoom %, trackpad-friendly interaction, controlled and uncontrolled viewport modes

0.subtitle: Motivation — Row Reshuffling Under Pan
0.0: Viewport-based stacking algorithms recompute the stack using only the items currently visible in the viewport. The same event ends up on row 3 at one pan position and row 1 at another, producing a visual jitter every time the user pans.
0.1: Packing rows once against the full dataset turns panning into a pure coordinate translation, so rows never shift. This became the core operating principle of the library.

1.subtitle: First-Fit Row Packing Algorithm
1.0: Each item's pixel footprint is the maximum of its dot diameter, range-bar length, and measured label width. After sorting by (start, label), a linear scan over the rowEnds array places each item in the lowest free row using first-fit interval partitioning.
1.1: Time complexity is O(n log n) for the sort plus O(n·R) for placement, with R typically in the 10–50 range so the sort term dominates. The resulting Map is fully deterministic — the same (items, pxPerMs) input always returns the same output.

2.subtitle: Precise Label Width Measurement with Canvas2D
2.0: Character-count-based estimates underrepresent Hangul width by roughly 30%, leading to visible label collisions. A measurer built on document.createElement('canvas').getContext('2d').measureText was introduced to obtain accurate pixel widths.
2.1: An SSR fallback returns the character-count estimate when document is undefined, ensuring the library runs identically on client and server. The measurer is exported so it can also be offloaded to a worker.

3.subtitle: Photoshop-Style Zoom % UX and Trackpad-Friendly Interaction
3.0: Zoom ranges from 100% (fit) up to 5000%. Cmd/Ctrl + wheel anchors at the cursor, while the toolbar +/− buttons anchor at the viewport center. The zoomFactor prop adjusts each zoom step's multiplier between 1.05 and 1.5.
3.1: Panning supports two-finger horizontal scroll, Shift + vertical wheel, and a 4px-threshold drag. Enabling zoomStable freezes the row layout at fit-zoom so rows remain identical at every zoom level as well.

0.subtitle: Same Event Moves to a Different Row When Panning
0.0: Problem: With viewport-based packing, the same event landed on different rows depending on pan position, producing visual jitter.
0.1:
Resolution

	1. Analyzed how viewport-based stack algorithms decide row placement.
	2. Replaced the algorithm with first-fit interval partitioning over the entire dataset.
	3. Separated zoom into its own axis and exposed a zoomStable prop so callers can opt into row stability under zoom too.
0.2: Result: All items now keep their row through pan, and zoomStable=true guarantees row stability through zoom as well.

1.subtitle: Hangul Label Width Underestimated by ~30%
1.0: Problem: Character-count-based label width estimates underrepresented Hangul's actual pixel width by about 30%, producing visual collisions between labels.
1.1:
Resolution

	1. Compared width measurements across various font and text combinations.
	2. Adopted Canvas2D measureText with the correct font context for accurate pixel widths.
	3. Added an automatic character-count fallback for SSR environments without a document object.
1.2: Result: Label widths are now measured correctly for Hangul, CJK, Latin, and mixed-script text, and label collisions disappeared.

0.1: Published a React timeline library with stable rows under pan and zoom to NPM
0.2: Eliminated label collisions for Hangul, CJK, and mixed-script text via Canvas2D-based width measurement
0.3: Exported the first-fit packing function and tick-picker so layouts can also be precomputed in a worker

1.1: Analyzing the visual flaw in viewport-based stacking sharpened the operating principle of the library end-to-end
1.2: Splitting packing and label measurement into separate modules secured compatibility with SSR and worker environments
1.3: Supporting both controlled and uncontrolled modes deepened understanding of synchronizing external viewport state

features.name: 📌 Stable Row Packing
features.description: First-fit packing over the full dataset keeps every item on the same row when panning

features.name: 🔤 Precise Label Width
features.description: Canvas2D measureText handles Hangul, CJK, and mixed-script text accurately

features.name: 🖱 Trackpad-Friendly
features.description: Two-finger horizontal scroll panning, Cmd/Ctrl + wheel anchored zoom, and click-and-drag panning

features.name: 🎚 Controlled / Uncontrolled
features.description: Two-way viewportStart/End binding and self-managed viewport are both supported

developmentProcess.title: Problem Identification
developmentProcess.content: Recognized the row-jitter problem caused by viewport-based stack algorithms under pan

developmentProcess.title: Core Algorithm Implementation
developmentProcess.content: Developed the core logic combining first-fit row packing over the full dataset with Canvas2D label width measurement

developmentProcess.title: NPM Publishing
developmentProcess.content: Published as a public npm package bundled with tsup and shipped with TypeScript type definitions

claudeInfo.details: Algorithm design, implementation, refactoring, and documentation were carried out using Claude Code CLI as a single agent. No harness was used; development was conducted through direct conversation.
